claims.text: Sanju Bhagat, a poor farmer from Nagpur, India, lived from his early 20s onward with a steadily growing abdominal bulge that his villagers mocked as making him look pregnant — and were unwittingly close to the truth.

evidence.quote:
sanju bhagat is a farmer from nagpur
india and his life was pretty normal
until his 20s when he started to develop
a small bulge in his stomach
as the years went on the bulge grew
larger and people from his village would
tease him that he looked like he was
pregnant
they didn't know how close to the truth
they were
[Music]
sanju's childhood was normal he had no
medical issues at least none that he was
aware of he never showed signs of a
tumor or any pain n

claims.rationale: Sanju Bhagat is a real, well-documented case widely covered by the BBC, NBC News, and multiple medical journals. Born in Nagpur, India; lived as a farmer with a growing abdominal mass. Biographical sketch is consistent with reporting.

claims.text: In 1999 at age 36 Sanju collapsed from breathing difficulty and was rushed to a Mumbai hospital, where Dr. Ajay Mehta diagnosed what he believed was a tumor large enough to be compressing Sanju's diaphragm.

evidence.quote:
trouble breathing so in 1999 at
the age of 36 sanju collapsed when he
couldn't catch his breath he was rushed
by an ambulance to a hospital in mumbai
dr ajay meadow was on duty that day and
immediately thought this was a tumor and
he said the tumor was so large that it
was pressing on sanju's diaphragm
disrupting his ability to breathe now
the doctors knew they had to remove the
tumor as quickly as possible to save
sanju's life now as they prepared for
surgery the medical staff noticed
something strange the tumor was actually
feeding

claims.rationale: Dr. Ajay Mehta at Tata Memorial Hospital in Mumbai (transcribed as 'meadow') performed the 1999 surgery. The transcript misspells the surgeon's name across mentions but the underlying historical event is well-attested in media interviews with Mehta himself.

claims.text: When Dr. Mehta opened Sanju's abdomen during emergency surgery, he discovered the mass was not a tumor but a deformed living fetus — Sanju's twin brother — with arms, legs, bones, hair, jaw, and teeth that had grown inside him for 36 years.

evidence.quote:
feeding on the contents of sanji's
stomach and when dr meta made the
incision gallons of fluid came out
he was then horrified to find this
wasn't a tumor at all
when dr metta tried to extract a large
tumor from sanji's stomach he said to my
surprise and horror i could shake hands
with somebody inside dr mehta realized
this wasn't a tumor at all it was
actually the mutated body of sanju's
twin brother the deformed fetus had arms
and legs bones hair a jaw teeth and

somehow sanju had carried the fetus
inside his body since birth and for 36
years it continued to live and grow now
this medical condition is very rare it's
called fetus infidu and it's defined as
the presence of one twin inside the body
of another which survives like a
parasite the fetus forms a cord like an
umbilical cord that leeches blood and
nutrients from its host now only 200
cases of fetus infetu have ever been
reported the earliest case occurred in
the early 19th cent

claims.rationale: The fetus-in-fetu finding and Mehta's now-famous 'I could shake hands with somebody inside' quote are accurate to the published surgical account. One small caution: framings like 'continued to live and grow for 36 years' are dramatic — fetus in fetu specimens have rudimentary growth driven by host blood supply but lack independent life in any meaningful sense; they are non-viable parasitic teratoma-adjacent twins.

claims.text: Fetus in fetu is an extremely rare congenital condition — only about 200 cases reported since the early 19th century, when German anatomist Johann Meckel first described it — in which a blastocyst divides unevenly during embryo development and one twin becomes encapsulated inside the other.

evidence.quote:
200
cases of fetus infetu have ever been
reported the earliest case occurred in
the early 19th century it was discovered
by johann meckel a german anatomist now
in that case a woman who insisted she
wasn't pregnant gave birth to a deformed
fetus and doctors soon learned that
she'd actually carried her twin her
entire life now fetus infeto happens
early in embryo development when it's
just a ball

of cells called a blastocyst
now it's rare but sometimes a blastocyst
divides and grows twin fetuses but in
this rare case they divide unevenly and
one fetus gets stuck inside the other
now most of the time the condition is
fatal to both twins which causes a
miscarriage but on some very rare
occasions one or both survive delivery
the condition usually goes unnoticed
until the parasitic twin grows so large
it actually causes discomfort in the
host as was the case with sanju bhagat
medical reports

claims.rationale: The 200-cases figure is consistent with medical literature (range typically reported 100-200 worldwide). Johann Friedrich Meckel the Younger did describe early cases in the 19th century. The blastocyst-uneven-division mechanism is the standard etiological hypothesis, though some pathologists treat fetus in fetu as a highly differentiated teratoma rather than a true monozygotic twinning event — the boundary is debated.

claims.text: After the surgery Sanju lost more than half his body weight — dropping from 200 to 88 pounds — and although doctors called the case a medical miracle, Sanju described his condition as a source of misery and shame rather than wonder.

evidence.quote:
after sanji was born and after
sanji recovered from the surgery he went
from 200 pounds to 88 pounds
even though the case was considered by
doctors to be a medical miracle sanju
said his condition was a source of
misery and shame sanju didn't make a
full recovery he's in good health and
now leads a normal life all his life
people had teased him about being
pregnant they didn't realize how right
t

claims.rationale: The dramatic weight drop is plausible given the size of the mass (gallons of fluid plus the parasitic fetus body) and is consistent with reporting; precise pound figures should be treated as approximate as media coverage has used varying numbers. Sanju's expressions of distress about his condition were captured in the BBC documentary 'Body Shock: The Man Who Was Pregnant' and are accurate.
